jowett.e: If you would explain to me a little more about them, perhaps I might be able to follow you.

turns.e: I mean, then, that the two which I select are the same which I mentioned before, the infinite and the finite. I will try to show that the infinite is, in a certain sense, many; the finite can wait.

jowett.e: Well, the two classes are the same which I mentioned before, one the finite, and the other the infinite; I will first show that the infinite is in a certain sense many, and the finite may be hereafter discussed.

turns.e: Yes.

jowett.e: I agree.

turns.e: Consider then. What I ask you to consider is difficult and debatable; but consider it all the same. In the first place, take hotter and colder and see whether you can conceive any limit of them, or whether the more and less which dwell in their very nature do not, so long as they continue to dwell therein, preclude the possibility of any end; for if there were any end of them, the more and less would themselves be ended.

jowett.e: And now consider well; for the question to which I invite your attention is difficult and controverted. When you speak of hotter and colder, can you conceive any limit in those qualities? Does not the more and less, which dwells in their very nature, prevent their having any end? for if they had an end, the more and less would themselves have an end.

turns.e: Very true.

jowett.e: That is most true.

turns.e: But always, we affirm, in the hotter and colder there is the more and less.

jowett.e: Ever, as we say, into the hotter and the colder there enters a more and a less.

turns.e: Certainly.

jowett.e: Yes.

turns.e: Always, then, the argument shows that these two have no end; and being endless, they are of course infinite.

jowett.e: Then, says the argument, there is never any end of them, and being endless they must also be infinite.

turns.e: Most emphatically, Socrates.

jowett.e: Yes, Socrates, that is exceedingly true.

turns.e: I am glad you responded, my dear Protarchus, and reminded me that the word emphatically which you have just used, and the word gently have the same force as more and less. For wherever they are present, they do not allow any definite quantity to exist; they always introduce in every instance a comparison—more emphatic than that which is quieter, or vice versa—and thus they create the relation of more and less, thereby doing away with fixed quantity. For, as I said just now, if they did not abolish quantity, but allowed it and measure to make their appearance in the abode of the more and less, the emphatically and gently, those latter would be banished from their own proper place. When once they had accepted definite quantity, they would no longer be hotter or colder; for hotter and colder are always progressing and never stationary; but quantity is at rest and does not progress. By this reasoning hotter and its opposite are shown to be infinite.

jowett.e: Yes, my dear Protarchus, and your answer reminds me that such an expression as 'exceedingly,' which you have just uttered, and also the term 'gently,' have the same significance as more or less; for whenever they occur they do not allow of the existence of quantity--they are always introducing degrees into actions, instituting a comparison of a more or a less excessive or a more or a less gentle, and at each creation of more or less, quantity disappears. For, as I was just now saying, if quantity and measure did not disappear, but were allowed to intrude in the sphere of more and less and the other comparatives, these last would be driven out of their own domain. When definite quantity is once admitted, there can be no longer a 'hotter' or a 'colder' (for these are always progressing, and are never in one stay); but definite quantity is at rest, and has ceased to progress. Which proves that comparatives, such as the hotter and the colder, are to be ranked in the class of the infinite.

turns.e: That appears to be the case, Socrates; but, as you said, these subjects are not easy to follow. Perhaps, however, continued repetition might lead to a satisfactory agreement between the questioner and him who is questioned.

jowett.e: Your remark certainly has the look of truth, Socrates; but these subjects, as you were saying, are difficult to follow at first. I think however, that if I could hear the argument repeated by you once or twice, there would be a substantial agreement between us.

turns.e: That is a good suggestion, and I must try to carry it out. However, to avoid waste of time in discussing all the individual examples, see if we can accept this as a designation of the infinite.

jowett.e: Yes, and I will try to meet your wish; but, as I would rather not waste time in the enumeration of endless particulars, let me know whether I may not assume as a note of the infinite--

turns.e: Accept what?

jowett.e: What?

turns.e: All things which appear to us to become more or less, or to admit of emphatic and gentle and excessive and the like, are to be put in the class of the infinite as their unity, in accordance with what we said a while ago, if you remember, that we ought to collect all things that are scattered and split up and impress upon them to the best of our ability the seal of some single nature.

jowett.e: I want to know whether such things as appear to us to admit of more or less, or are denoted by the words 'exceedingly,' 'gently,' 'extremely,' and the like, may not be referred to the class of the infinite, which is their unity, for, as was asserted in the previous argument, all things that were divided and dispersed should be brought together, and have the mark or seal of some one nature, if possible, set upon them--do you remember?

turns.e: I remember.

jowett.e: Yes.

turns.e: And the things which do not admit of more and less and the like, but do admit of all that is opposed to them—first equality and the equal, then the double, and anything which is a definite number or measure in relation to such a number or measure— all these might properly be assigned to the class of the finite. What do you say to that?

jowett.e: And all things which do not admit of more or less, but admit their opposites, that is to say, first of all, equality, and the equal, or again, the double, or any other ratio of number and measure--all these may, I think, be rightly reckoned by us in the class of the limited or finite; what do you say?

turns.e: Excellent, Socrates.

jowett.e: Excellent, Socrates.

turns.e: Well, what shall we say is the nature of the third class, made by combining these two?

jowett.e: And now what nature shall we ascribe to the third or compound kind?

turns.e: You will tell me, I fancy, by answering your own question.

jowett.e: You, I think, will have to tell me that.

turns.e: Nay, a god will do so, if any god will give ear to my prayers.

jowett.e: Rather God will tell you, if there be any God who will listen to my prayers.

turns.e: Pray, then, and watch.

jowett.e: Offer up a prayer, then, and think.

turns.e: I am watching; and I think, Protarchus, one of the gods has this moment been gracious unto me.

jowett.e: I am thinking, Protarchus, and I believe that some God has befriended us.

turns.e: What do you mean, and what evidence have you?

jowett.e: What do you mean, and what proof have you to offer of what you are saying?

turns.e: I will tell you, of course. Just follow what I say.

jowett.e: I will tell you, and do you listen to my words.

turns.e: Say on.

jowett.e: Proceed.

turns.e: We spoke just now of hotter and colder, did we not?

jowett.e: Were we not speaking just now of hotter and colder?

turns.e: Yes.

jowett.e: True.

turns.e: Add to them drier and wetter, more and less, quicker and slower, greater and smaller, and all that we assigned before to the class which unites more and less.

jowett.e: Add to them drier, wetter, more, less, swifter, slower, greater, smaller, and all that in the preceding argument we placed under the unity of more and less.

turns.e: You mean the class of the infinite?

jowett.e: In the class of the infinite, you mean?

turns.e: Yes. Mix with that the second class, the offspring of the limit.

jowett.e: Yes; and now mingle this with the other.

turns.e: What class do you mean?

jowett.e: What is the other.

turns.e: The class of the finite, which we ought just now to have reduced to unity, as we did that of the infinite. We have not done that, but perhaps we shall even now accomplish the same end, if these two are both unified and then the third class is revealed.

jowett.e: The class of the finite which we ought to have brought together as we did the infinite; but, perhaps, it will come to the same thing if we do so now;--when the two are combined, a third will appear.

turns.e: What third class, and what do you mean?

jowett.e: What do you mean by the class of the finite?

turns.e: The class of the equal and double and everything which puts an end to the differences between opposites and makes them commensurable and harmonious by the introduction of number.

jowett.e: The class of the equal and the double, and any class which puts an end to difference and opposition, and by introducing number creates harmony and proportion among the different elements.

turns.e: I understand. I think you mean that by mixture of these elements certain results are produced in each instance.

jowett.e: I understand; you seem to me to mean that the various opposites, when you mingle with them the class of the finite, takes certain forms.

turns.e: Yes, you are right.

jowett.e: Yes, that is my meaning.

turns.e: Go on.

jowett.e: Proceed.

turns.e: In cases of illness, does not the proper combination of these elements produce health?

jowett.e: Does not the right participation in the finite give health--in disease, for instance?

turns.e: Certainly.

jowett.e: Certainly.

turns.e: And in the acute and the grave, the quick and the slow, which are unlimited, the addition of these same elements creates a limit and establishes the whole art of music in all its perfection, does it not?

jowett.e: And whereas the high and low, the swift and the slow are infinite or unlimited, does not the addition of the principles aforesaid introduce a limit, and perfect the whole frame of music?

turns.e: Excellent.

jowett.e: Yes, certainly.

turns.e: And again in the case of cold and hot weather, the introduction of these elements removes the excess and indefiniteness and creates moderation and harmony.

jowett.e: Or, again, when cold and heat prevail, does not the introduction of them take away excess and indefiniteness, and infuse moderation and harmony?

turns.e: Assuredly.

jowett.e: Certainly.

turns.e: And thence arise the seasons and all the beauties of our world, by mixture of the infinite with the finite?

jowett.e: And from a like admixture of the finite and infinite come the seasons, and all the delights of life?

turns.e: Of course.

jowett.e: Most true.

turns.e: There are countless other things which I pass over, such as health, beauty, and strength of the body and the many glorious beauties of the soul. For this goddess, my fair Philebus, beholding the violence and universal wickedness which prevailed, since there was no limit of pleasures or of indulgence in them, established law and order, which contain a limit. You say she did harm; I say, on the contrary, she brought salvation. What do you think, Protarchus?

jowett.e: I omit ten thousand other things, such as beauty and health and strength, and the many beauties and high perfections of the soul: O my beautiful Philebus, the goddess, methinks, seeing the universal wantonness and wickedness of all things, and that there was in them no limit to pleasures and self-indulgence, devised the limit of law and order, whereby, as you say, Philebus, she torments, or as I maintain, delivers the soul.--What think you, Protarchus?

turns.e: What you say, Socrates, pleases me greatly.

jowett.e: Her ways are much to my mind, Socrates.

turns.e: I have spoken of these three classes, you observe.

jowett.e: You will observe that I have spoken of three classes?

turns.e: Yes, I believe I understand; I think you mean that the infinite is one class and the finite is another class among existing things; but what you wish to designate as the third class, I do not comprehend very well.

jowett.e: Yes, I think that I understand you: you mean to say that the infinite is one class, and that the finite is a second class of existences; but what you would make the third I am not so certain.

turns.e: No, because the multitude which springs up in the third class overpowers you and yet the infinite also comprised many classes, nevertheless, since they were sealed with the seal of the more and less, they were seen to be of one class.

jowett.e: That is because the amazing variety of the third class is too much for you, my dear friend; but there was not this difficulty with the infinite, which also comprehended many classes, for all of them were sealed with the note of more and less, and therefore appeared one.

turns.e: True.

jowett.e: True.

turns.e: And the finite, again, did not contain many classes, nor were we disturbed about its natural unity.

jowett.e: And the finite or limit had not many divisions, and we readily acknowledged it to be by nature one?

turns.e: Of course not.

jowett.e: Yes.

turns.e: No, not at all. And as to the third class, understand that I mean every offspring of these two which comes into being as a result of the measures created by the cooperation of the finite.

jowett.e: Yes, indeed; and when I speak of the third class, understand me to mean any offspring of these, being a birth into true being, effected by the measure which the limit introduces.

turns.e: I understand.

jowett.e: I understand.

turns.e: But we said there was, in addition to three classes, a fourth to be investigated. Let us do that together. See whether you think that everything which comes into being must necessarily come into being through a cause.

jowett.e: Still there was, as we said, a fourth class to be investigated, and you must assist in the investigation; for does not everything which comes into being, of necessity come into being through a cause?

turns.e: Yes, I do; for how could it come into being apart from a cause?

jowett.e: Yes, certainly; for how can there be anything which has no cause?

turns.e: Does not the nature of that which makes or creates differ only in name from the cause, and may not the creative agent and the cause be properly considered one?

jowett.e: And is not the agent the same as the cause in all except name; the agent and the cause may be rightly called one?

turns.e: Yes.

jowett.e: Very true.

turns.e: And, again, we shall find that, on the same principle, that which is made or created differs in name only from that which comes into being, shall we not?

jowett.e: And the same may be said of the patient, or effect; we shall find that they too differ, as I was saying, only in name--shall we not?

turns.e: We shall.

jowett.e: We shall.

turns.e: And the creative agent always naturally leads, and that which is created follows after it as it comes into being?

jowett.e: The agent or cause always naturally leads, and the patient or effect naturally follows it?

turns.e: Certainly.

jowett.e: Certainly.

turns.e: Then the cause and that which is the servant of the cause for the purpose of generation are not the same.

jowett.e: Then the cause and what is subordinate to it in generation are not the same, but different?

turns.e: Of course not.

jowett.e: True.

turns.e: Did not the things which come into being and the things out of which they come into being furnish us all the three classes?

jowett.e: Did not the things which were generated, and the things out of which they were generated, furnish all the three classes?

turns.e: Certainly.

jowett.e: Yes.

turns.e: And that which produces all these, the cause, we call the fourth, as it has been satisfactorily shown to be distinct from the others?

jowett.e: And the creator or cause of them has been satisfactorily proven to be distinct from them,--and may therefore be called a fourth principle?

turns.e: Yes, it is distinct.

jowett.e: So let us call it.

turns.e: It is, then, proper, now that we have distinguished the four, to make sure that we remember them separately by enumerating them in order.

jowett.e: Quite right; but now, having distinguished the four, I think that we had better refresh our memories by recapitulating each of them in order.

turns.e: Yes, certainly.

jowett.e: By all means.

turns.e: The first, then, I call infinite, the second limit or finite, and the third something generated by a mixture of these two. And should I be making any mistake if I called the cause of this mixture and creation the fourth?

jowett.e: Then the first I will call the infinite or unlimited, and the second the finite or limited; then follows the third, an essence compound and generated; and I do not think that I shall be far wrong in speaking of the cause of mixture and generation as the fourth.

turns.e: Certainly not.

jowett.e: Certainly not.

turns.e: Now what is the next step in our argument, and what was our purpose in coming to the point we have reached? Was it not this? We were trying to find out whether the second place belonged to pleasure or to wisdom, were we not?

jowett.e: And now what is the next question, and how came we hither? Were we not enquiring whether the second place belonged to pleasure or wisdom?

turns.e: Yes, we were.

jowett.e: We were.

turns.e: And may we not, perhaps, now that we have finished with these points, be better able to come to a decision about the first and second places, which was the original subject of our discussion?

jowett.e: And now, having determined these points, shall we not be better able to decide about the first and second place, which was the original subject of dispute?

turns.e: Perhaps.

jowett.e: I dare say.

turns.e: Well then; we decided that the mixed life of pleasure and wisdom was the victor, did we not?

jowett.e: We said, if you remember, that the mixed life of pleasure and wisdom was the conqueror--did we not?

turns.e: Yes.

jowett.e: True.

turns.e: And do we not see what kind of life this is, and to what class it belongs?

jowett.e: And we see what is the place and nature of this life and to what class it is to be assigned?

turns.e: Of course we do.

jowett.e: Beyond a doubt.

turns.e: We shall say that it belongs to the third class; for that class is not formed by mixture of any two things, but of all the things which belong to the infinite, bound by the finite; and therefore this victorious life would rightly be considered a part of this class.

jowett.e: This is evidently comprehended in the third or mixed class; which is not composed of any two particular ingredients, but of all the elements of infinity, bound down by the finite, and may therefore be truly said to comprehend the conqueror life.

turns.e: Quite rightly.

jowett.e: Most true.

turns.e: Well then, what of your life, Philebus, of unmixed pleasure? In which of the aforesaid classes may it properly be said to belong? But before you tell me, please answer this question.

jowett.e: And what shall we say, Philebus, of your life which is all sweetness; and in which of the aforesaid classes is that to be placed? Perhaps you will allow me to ask you a question before you answer?

turns.e: Ask your question.

jowett.e: Let me hear.

turns.e: Have pleasure and pain a limit, or are they among the things which admit of more and less?

jowett.e: Have pleasure and pain a limit, or do they belong to the class which admits of more and less?

turns.e: Yes, they are among those which admit of the more, Socrates; for pleasure would not be absolute good if it were not infinite in number and degree.

jowett.e: They belong to the class which admits of more, Socrates; for pleasure would not be perfectly good if she were not infinite in quantity and degree.

turns.e: Nor would pain, Philebus, be absolute evil; so it is not the infinite which supplies any element of good in pleasure; we must look for something else. Well, I grant you that pleasure and pain are in the class of the infinite but to which of the aforesaid classes, Protarchus and Philebus, can we now without irreverence assign wisdom, knowledge, and mind? I think we must find the right answer to this question, for our danger is great if we fail.

jowett.e: Nor would pain, Philebus, be perfectly evil. And therefore the infinite cannot be that element which imparts to pleasure some degree of good. But now--admitting, if you like, that pleasure is of the nature of the infinite--in which of the aforesaid classes, O Protarchus and Philebus, can we without irreverence place wisdom and knowledge and mind? And let us be careful, for I think that the danger will be very serious if we err on this point.

turns.e: Oh Socrates, you exalt your own god.

jowett.e: You magnify, Socrates, the importance of your favourite god.

turns.e: And you your goddess, my friend. But the question calls for an answer, all the same.

jowett.e: And you, my friend, are also magnifying your favourite goddess; but still I must beg you to answer the question.

turns.e: Socrates is right, Philebus; you ought to do as he asks.

jowett.e: Socrates is quite right, Philebus, and we must submit to him.

turns.e: Did you not, Protarchus, elect to reply in my place?

jowett.e: And did not you, Protarchus, propose to answer in my place?

turns.e: Yes; but now I am somewhat at a loss, and I ask you, Socrates, to be our spokesman yourself, that we may not select the wrong representative and so say something improper.

jowett.e: Certainly I did; but I am now in a great strait, and I must entreat you, Socrates, to be our spokesman, and then we shall not say anything wrong or disrespectful of your favourite.

turns.e: I must do as you ask, Protarchus; and it is not difficult. But did I really, as Philebus said, embarrass you by playfully exalting my god, when I asked to what class mind and knowledge should be assigned?

jowett.e: I must obey you, Protarchus; nor is the task which you impose a difficult one; but did I really, as Philebus implies, disconcert you with my playful solemnity, when I asked the question to what class mind and knowledge belong?

turns.e: You certainly did, Socrates.

jowett.e: You did, indeed, Socrates.

turns.e: Yet the answer is easy; for all philosophers agree—whereby they really exalt themselves—that mind is king of heaven and earth. Perhaps they are right. But let us, if you please, investigate the question of its class more at length.

jowett.e: Yet the answer is easy, since all philosophers assert with one voice that mind is the king of heaven and earth--in reality they are magnifying themselves. And perhaps they are right. But still I should like to consider the class of mind, if you do not object, a little more fully.

turns.e: Speak just as you like, Socrates. Do not consider length, so far as we are concerned you cannot bore us.
Good. Then let us begin by asking a question.

jowett.e: Very good; let us begin then, Protarchus, by asking a question.

turns.e: What is the question?

jowett.e: What question?

turns.e: Shall we say, Protarchus, that all things and this which is called the universe are governed by an irrational and fortuitous power and mere chance, or, on the contrary, as our forefathers said, are ordered and directed by mind and a marvellous wisdom?

jowett.e: Whether all this which they call the universe is left to the guidance of unreason and chance medley, or, on the contrary, as our fathers have declared, ordered and governed by a marvellous intelligence and wisdom.

turns.e: The two points of view have nothing in common, my wonderful Socrates. For what you are now saying seems to me actually impious. But the assertion that mind orders all things is worthy of the aspect of the world, of sun, moon, stars, and the whole revolving universe; I can never say or think anything else about it.

jowett.e: Wide asunder are the two assertions, illustrious Socrates, for that which you were just now saying to me appears to be blasphemy; but the other assertion, that mind orders all things, is worthy of the aspect of the world, and of the sun, and of the moon, and of the stars and of the whole circle of the heavens; and never will I say or think otherwise.

turns.e: Do you, then, think we should assent to this and agree in the doctrine of our predecessors, not merely intending to repeat the words of others, with no risk to ourselves, but ready to share with them in the risk and the blame, if any clever man declares that this world is not thus ordered, but is without order?

jowett.e: Shall we then agree with them of old time in maintaining this doctrine,--not merely reasserting the notions of others, without risk to ourselves,--but shall we share in the danger, and take our part of the reproach which will await us, when an ingenious individual declares that all is disorder?

turns.e: Yes, of course I do.

jowett.e: That would certainly be my wish.

turns.e: Then observe the argument that now comes against us.

jowett.e: Then now please to consider the next stage of the argument.

turns.e: Go on.

jowett.e: Let me hear.

turns.e: We see the elements which belong to the natures of all living beings, fire, water, air, and earth—or, as the storm-tossed mariners say, land in sight— in the constitution of the universe.

jowett.e: We see that the elements which enter into the nature of the bodies of all animals, fire, water, air, and, as the storm-tossed sailor cries, 'land' (i.e., earth), reappear in the constitution of the world.

turns.e: Certainly and we are truly storm-tossed in the puzzling cross-currents of this discussion.

jowett.e: The proverb may be applied to us; for truly the storm gathers over us, and we are at our wit's end.

turns.e: Well, here is a point for you to consider in relation to each of these elements as it exists in us.

jowett.e: There is something to be remarked about each of these elements.

turns.e: What is the point?

jowett.e: What is it?

turns.e: Each element in us is small and poor and in no way pure at all or endowed with the power which is worthy of its nature. Take one example and apply it to all. Fire, for instance, exists in us and also in the universe.

jowett.e: Only a small fraction of any one of them exists in us, and that of a mean sort, and not in any way pure, or having any power worthy of its nature. One instance will prove this of all of them; there is fire within us, and in the universe.

turns.e: Of course.

jowett.e: True.

turns.e: And that which is in us is small, weak, and poor, but that which is in the universe is marvellous in quantity, beauty, and every power which belongs to fire.

jowett.e: And is not our fire small and weak and mean? But the fire in the universe is wonderful in quantity and beauty, and in every power that fire has.

turns.e: What you say is very true.

jowett.e: Most true.

turns.e: Well, is the fire of the universe nourished, originated, and ruled by the fire within us, or, on the contrary, does my fire, and yours, and that of all living beings derive nourishment and all that from the universal fire?

jowett.e: And is the fire in the universe nourished and generated and ruled by the fire in us, or is the fire in you and me, and in other animals, dependent on the universal fire?

turns.e: That question does not even deserve an answer.

jowett.e: That is a question which does not deserve an answer.

turns.e: True; and you will, I fancy, say the same of the earth which is in us living creatures and that which is in the universe, and concerning all the other elements about which I asked a moment ago your answer will be the same.

jowett.e: Right; and you would say the same, if I am not mistaken, of the earth which is in animals and the earth which is in the universe, and you would give a similar reply about all the other elements?

turns.e: Yes. Who could answer otherwise without being called a lunatic?

jowett.e: Why, how could any man who gave any other be deemed in his senses?

turns.e: Nobody, I fancy. Now follow the next step. When we see that all the aforesaid elements are gathered together into a unit, do we not call them a body?

jowett.e: I do not think that he could--but now go on to the next step. When we saw those elements of which we have been speaking gathered up in one, did we not call them a body?

turns.e: Of course.

jowett.e: We did.

turns.e: Apply the same line of thought to that which we call the universe. It would likewise be a body, being composed of the same elements.

jowett.e: And the same may be said of the cosmos, which for the same reason may be considered to be a body, because made up of the same elements.

turns.e: Quite right.

jowett.e: Very true.

turns.e: Does our body derive, obtain, and possess from that body, or that body from ours, nourishment and everything else that we mentioned just now?

jowett.e: But is our body nourished wholly by this body, or is this body nourished by our body, thence deriving and having the qualities of which we were just now speaking?

turns.e: That, Socrates, is another question not worth asking.

jowett.e: That again, Socrates, is a question which does not deserve to be asked.

turns.e: Well, is this next one worth asking? What will you say to it?

jowett.e: Well, tell me, is this question worth asking?

turns.e: What is it?

jowett.e: What question?

turns.e: Shall we not say that our body has a soul?

jowett.e: May our body be said to have a soul?

turns.e: Clearly we shall.

jowett.e: Clearly.

turns.e: Where did it get it, Protarchus, unless the body of the universe had a soul, since that body has the same elements as ours, only in every way superior?

jowett.e: And whence comes that soul, my dear Protarchus, unless the body of the universe, which contains elements like those in our bodies but in every way fairer, had also a soul? Can there be another source?

turns.e: Clearly it could get it from no other source.

jowett.e: Clearly, Socrates, that is the only source.

turns.e: No; for we surely do not believe, Protarchus, that of those four elements, the finite, the infinite, the combination, and the element of cause which exists in all things, this last, which gives to our bodies souls and the art of physical exercise and medical treatment when the body is ill, and which is in general a composing and healing power, is called the sum of all wisdom, and yet, while these same elements exist in the entire heaven and in great parts thereof, and area moreover, fair and pure, there is no means of including among them that nature which is the fairest and most precious of all.

jowett.e: And suppose you part off from pleasures and pains the element which makes them appear to be greater or less than they really are: you will acknowledge that this element is illusory, and you will never say that the corresponding excess or defect of pleasure or pain is real or true.

turns.e: No, certainly not.

turns.e: Certain men who are said to be master thinkers about nature, and who deny the existence of pleasures altogether.

turns.e: Do you recommend that we adopt their view, Socrates?

jowett.e: And would you, Socrates, have us agree with them?

turns.e: No, but that we make use of them as seers who divine the truth, not by acquired skill, but by some innate and not ignoble repugnance which makes them hate the power of pleasure and think it so utterly unsound that its very attractiveness is mere trickery, not pleasure. You may make use of them in this way, considering also their other expressions of dislike; and after that you shall learn of the pleasures which seem to me to be true, in order that we may consider the power of pleasure from both points of view and form our judgement by comparing them.

jowett.e: Why, no, I would rather use them as a sort of diviners, who divine the truth, not by rules of art, but by an instinctive repugnance and extreme detestation which a noble nature has of the power of pleasure, in which they think that there is nothing sound, and her seductive influence is declared by them to be witchcraft, and not pleasure. This is the use which you may make of them. And when you have considered the various grounds of their dislike, you shall hear from me what I deem to be true pleasures. Having thus examined the nature of pleasure from both points of view, we will bring her up for judgment.

turns.e: You are right.

turns.e: Let us, then, consider these men as allies and follow them in the track of their dislike. Now you, Protarchus, must reply to them as you have been replying to me.

jowett.e: Then let us enter into an alliance with these philosophers and follow in the track of their dislike. turns.e: By all means, and I say to them that we should look at the greatest things.

turns.e: Then should we appear to be right in saying that if we wished to discover the greatest pleasures we should have to look, not at health, but at disease? jowett.e: Well, then, shall we not be right in saying, that if a person would wish to see the greatest pleasures he ought to go and look, not at health, but at disease? jowett.e: You will soon have a better opportunity of showing whether you do or not, Protarchus. Answer now, and tell me whether you see, I will not say more, but more intense and excessive pleasures in wantonness than in temperance? Reflect before you speak.

jowett.e: I understand you, and see that there is a great difference between them; the temperate are restrained by the wise man's aphorism of 'Never too much,' which is their rule, but excess of pleasure possessing the minds of fools and wantons becomes madness and makes them shout with delight.

turns.e: Good; and if that is true, it is clear that the greatest pleasures and the greatest pains originate in some depravity of soul and body, not in virtue.

turns.e: Some mixtures are concerned with the body and are in the body only, and some belong only to the soul and are in the soul; and we shall also find some mingled pains and pleasures belonging both to the soul and to the body, and these are sometimes called pleasures, sometimes pains.

jowett.e: There are some mixtures which are of the body, and only in the body, and others which are of the soul, and only in the soul; while there are other mixtures of pleasures with pains, common both to soul and body, which in their composite state are called sometimes pleasures and sometimes pains.

turns.e: How so?

jowett.e: How is that?

turns.e: Whenever, in the process of restoration or destruction, anyone has two opposite feelings, as we sometimes are cold, but are growing warm, or are hot, but are growing cold, the desire of having the one and being free from the other, the mixture of bitter and sweet, as they say, joined with the difficulty in getting rid of the bitter, produces impatience and, later, wild excitement.

jowett.e: Whenever, in the restoration or in the derangement of nature, a man experiences two opposite feelings; for example, when he is cold and is growing warm, or again, when he is hot and is becoming cool, and he wants to have the one and be rid of the other;--the sweet has a bitter, as the common saying is, and both together fasten upon him and create irritation and in time drive him to distraction.

turns.e: What you say is perfectly true.

jowett.e: That description is very true to nature.

turns.e: And such mixtures sometimes consist of equal pains and pleasures and sometimes contain more of one or the other, do they not?

jowett.e: And in these sorts of mixtures the pleasures and pains are sometimes equal, and sometimes one or other of them predominates?

turns.e: Of course.

jowett.e: True.

turns.e: In the case of the mixtures in which the pains are more than the pleasures—say the itch, which we mentioned just now, or tickling—when the burning inflammation is within and is not reached by the rubbing and scratching, which separate only such mixtures as are on the surface, sometimes by bringing the affected parts to the fire or to something cold we change from wretchedness to inexpressible pleasures, and sometimes the opposition between the internal and the external produces a mixture of pains and pleasures, whichever happens to preponderate; this is the result of the forcible separation of combined elements, or the combination of those that were separate, and the concomitant juxtaposition of pains and pleasures.

jowett.e: Of cases in which the pain exceeds the pleasure, an example is afforded by itching, of which we were just now speaking, and by the tingling which we feel when the boiling and fiery element is within, and the rubbing and motion only relieves the surface, and does not reach the parts affected; then if you put them to the fire, and as a last resort apply cold to them, you may often produce the most intense pleasure or pain in the inner parts, which contrasts and mingles with the pain or pleasure, as the case may be, of the outer parts; and this is due to the forcible separation of what is united, or to the union of what is separated, and to the juxtaposition of pleasure and pain.

turns.e: Very true.

jowett.e: Quite so.

turns.e: And when the pleasure is the predominant element in the mixture, the slight tincture of pain tickles a man and makes him mildly impatient, or again an excessive proportion of pleasure excites him and sometimes even makes him leap for joy; it produces in him all sorts of colors, attitudes, and paintings, and even causes great amazement and foolish shouting, does it not?

jowett.e: Sometimes the element of pleasure prevails in a man, and the slight undercurrent of pain makes him tingle, and causes a gentle irritation; or again, the excessive infusion of pleasure creates an excitement in him,--he even leaps for joy, he assumes all sorts of attitudes, he changes all manner of colours, he gasps for breath, and is quite amazed, and utters the most irrational exclamations.

turns.e: Certainly.

jowett.e: Yes, indeed.

turns.e: And it makes him say of himself, and others say of him, that he is pleased to death with these delights, and the more unrestrained and foolish he is, the more he always gives himself up to the pursuit of these pleasures; he calls them the greatest of all things and counts that man the happiest who lives most entirely in the enjoyment of them.

jowett.e: He will say of himself, and others will say of him, that he is dying with these delights; and the more dissipated and good-for-nothing he is, the more vehemently he pursues them in every way; of all pleasures he declares them to be the greatest; and he reckons him who lives in the most constant enjoyment of them to be the happiest of mankind.

turns.e: Socrates, you have described admirably what happens in the case of most people.

jowett.e: That, Socrates, is a very true description of the opinions of the majority about pleasures.

turns.e: That may be, Protarchus, so far as concerns purely bodily pleasures in which internal and external sensations unite; but concerning the pleasures in which the soul and the body contribute opposite elements, each adding pain or pleasure to the other’s pleasure or pain, so that both unite in a single mixture—concerning these I said before that when a man is empty he desires to be filled, and rejoices in his expectation, but is pained by his emptiness, and now I add, what I did not say at that time, that in all these cases, which are innumerable, of opposition between soul and body, there is one single mixture of pain and pleasure.

jowett.e: Yes, Protarchus, quite true of the mixed pleasures, which arise out of the communion of external and internal sensations in the body; there are also cases in which the mind contributes an opposite element to the body, whether of pleasure or pain, and the two unite and form one mixture. Concerning these I have already remarked, that when a man is empty he desires to be full, and has pleasure in hope and pain in vacuity. But now I must further add what I omitted before, that in all these and similar emotions in which body and mind are opposed (and they are innumerable), pleasure and pain coalesce in one.

turns.e: How do you mean? I am afraid I can never do it.

turns.e: Then you say that I must now make the division?

jowett.e: Do you mean to say that I must make the division for you?

turns.e: Yes, I say so, and I beg you to do so, besides.

jowett.e: Yes, and what is more, I beg that you will.

turns.e: Must not all those who do not know themselves be affected by their condition in one of three ways?

jowett.e: Are there not three ways in which ignorance of self may be shown?

turns.e: How is that?

jowett.e: What are they?

turns.e: First in regard to wealth; such a man thinks he is richer than he is.

jowett.e: In the first place, about money; the ignorant may fancy himself richer than he is.

turns.e: Certainly a good many are affected in that way.

jowett.e: Yes, that is a very common error.

turns.e: And there are still more who think they are taller and handsomer than they are and that they possess better physical qualities in general than is the case.

jowett.e: And still more often he will fancy that he is taller or fairer than he is, or that he has some other advantage of person which he really has not.

turns.e: Certainly.

jowett.e: Of course.

turns.e: But by far the greatest number, I fancy, err in the third way, about the qualities of, the soul, thinking that they excel in virtue when they do not.

jowett.e: And yet surely by far the greatest number err about the goods of the mind; they imagine themselves to be much better men than they are.

turns.e: Yes, most decidedly.

jowett.e: Yes, that is by far the commonest delusion.

turns.e: And of all the virtues, is not wisdom the one to which people in general lay claim, thereby filling themselves with strife and false conceit of wisdom?

jowett.e: And of all the virtues, is not wisdom the one which the mass of mankind are always claiming, and which most arouses in them a spirit of contention and lying conceit of wisdom?

turns.e: Yes, to be sure.

jowett.e: Certainly.

turns.e: And we should surely be right in calling all that an evil condition.

jowett.e: And may not all this be truly called an evil condition?

turns.e: Very much so.

jowett.e: Very evil.

turns.e: Then this must further be divided into two parts, if we are to gain insight into childish envy with its absurd mixture of pleasure and pain. How shall we divide it, do you say? All who have this false and foolish conceit of themselves fall, like the rest of mankind, into two classes: some necessarily have strength and power, others, as I believe, the reverse.

jowett.e: All who are silly enough to entertain this lying conceit of themselves may of course be divided, like the rest of mankind, into two classes--one having power and might; and the other the reverse.

turns.e: Yes, necessarily.

jowett.e: Certainly.

turns.e: Make the division, then, on that principle; those of them who have this false conceit and are weak and unable to revenge themselves when they are laughed at you may truly call ridiculous, but those who are strong and able to revenge themselves you will define most correctly to yourself by calling them powerful, terrible, and hateful, for ignorance in the powerful is hateful and infamous—since whether real or feigned it injures their neighbors—but ignorance in the weak appears to us as naturally ridiculous.

jowett.e: Let this, then, be the principle of division; those of them who are weak and unable to revenge themselves, when they are laughed at, may be truly called ridiculous, but those who can defend themselves may be more truly described as strong and formidable; for ignorance in the powerful is hateful and horrible, because hurtful to others both in reality and in fiction, but powerless ignorance may be reckoned, and in truth is, ridiculous.

turns.e: Quite right. But the mixture of pleasure and pain in all this is not yet clear to me.

jowett.e: That is very true, but I do not as yet see where is the admixture of pleasures and pains.

turns.e: First, then, take up the nature of envy.

jowett.e: Well, then, let us examine the nature of envy.

turns.e: Go on.

jowett.e: Proceed.

turns.e: Is envy a kind of unrighteous pain and also a pleasure?

jowett.e: Is not envy an unrighteous pleasure, and also an unrighteous pain?

turns.e: Undoubtedly.

jowett.e: Most true.

turns.e: But it is neither wrong nor envious to rejoice in the misfortunes of our enemies, is it?

jowett.e: There is nothing envious or wrong in rejoicing at the misfortunes of enemies?

turns.e: No, of course not.

jowett.e: Certainly not.

turns.e: But when people sometimes see the misfortunes of their friends and rejoice instead of grieving, is not that wrong?

jowett.e: But to feel joy instead of sorrow at the sight of our friends' misfortunes--is not that wrong?

turns.e: Of course it is.

jowett.e: Undoubtedly.

turns.e: And we said that ignorance was an evil to every one, did we not?

jowett.e: Did we not say that ignorance was always an evil?

turns.e: True.

jowett.e: True.

turns.e: Then the false conceits of our friends concerning their wisdom, their beauty, and their other qualities which we mentioned just now, saying that they belong to three classes, are ridiculous when they are weak, but hateful when they are powerful. Shall we, or shall we not, affirm that, as I said just now, this state of mind when possessed in its harmless form by any of our friends, is ridiculous in the eyes of others?

jowett.e: And the three kinds of vain conceit in our friends which we enumerated--the vain conceit of beauty, of wisdom, and of wealth, are ridiculous if they are weak, and detestable when they are powerful: May we not say, as I was saying before, that our friends who are in this state of mind, when harmless to others, are simply ridiculous?

turns.e: Certainly it is ridiculous.

jowett.e: They are ridiculous.

turns.e: And do we not agree that ignorance is in itself a misfortune?

jowett.e: And do we not acknowledge this ignorance of theirs to be a misfortune?

turns.e: Yes, a great one.

jowett.e: Certainly.

turns.e: And do we feel pleasure or pain when we laugh at it?

jowett.e: And do we feel pain or pleasure in laughing at it?

turns.e: Pleasure, evidently.

jowett.e: Clearly we feel pleasure.

turns.e: Did we not say that pleasure in the misfortunes of friends was caused by envy?

jowett.e: And was not envy the source of this pleasure which we feel at the misfortunes of friends?

turns.e: There can be no other cause.

jowett.e: Certainly.

turns.e: Then our argument declares that when we laugh at the ridiculous qualities of our friends, we mix pleasure with pain, since we mix it with envy; for we have agreed all along that envy is a pain of the soul, and that laughter is a pleasure, yet these two are present at the same time on such occasions.

jowett.e: Then the argument shows that when we laugh at the folly of our friends, pleasure, in mingling with envy, mingles with pain, for envy has been acknowledged by us to be mental pain, and laughter is pleasant; and so we envy and laugh at the same instant.

turns.e: True.

jowett.e: True.

turns.e: So now our argument shows that in mournings and tragedies and comedies, not merely on the stage, but in all the tragedy and comedy of life, and in countless other ways, pain is mixed with pleasure.

jowett.e: And the argument implies that there are combinations of pleasure and pain in lamentations, and in tragedy and comedy, not only on the stage, but on the greater stage of human life; and so in endless other cases.

turns.e: It is impossible not to agree with that, Socrates, even though one be most eager to maintain the opposite opinion.

jowett.e: I do not see how any one can deny what you say, Socrates, however eager he may be to assert the opposite opinion.

turns.e: Again we mentioned anger, yearning, mourning, love, jealousy, envy, and the like, as conditions in which we should find a mixture of the two elements we have now often named, did we not?

jowett.e: I mentioned anger, desire, sorrow, fear, love, emulation, envy, and similar emotions, as examples in which we should find a mixture of the two elements so often named; did I not?

turns.e: Yes.

jowett.e: Yes.

turns.e: And we understand that all the details I have been describing just now are concerned only with sorrow and envy and anger?

jowett.e: We may observe that our conclusions hitherto have had reference only to sorrow and envy and anger.

turns.e: Of course we understand that.

jowett.e: I see.

turns.e: Then there are still many others of those conditions left for us to discuss.

jowett.e: Then many other cases still remain?

turns.e: Yes, very many.

jowett.e: Certainly.

turns.e: Now why do you particularly suppose I pointed out to you the mixture of pain and pleasure in comedy? Was it not for the sake of convincing you, because it is easy to show the mixture in love and fear and the rest, and because I thought that when you had made this example your own, you would relieve me from the necessity of discussing those other conditions in detail, and would simply accept the fact that in the affections of the body apart from the soul, of the soul apart from the body, and of the two in common, there are plentiful mixtures of pain and pleasure? So tell me; will you let me off, or will you keep on till midnight? But I think I need say only a few words to induce you to let me off. I will agree to give you an account of all these matters tomorrow, but now I wish to steer my bark towards the remaining points that are needful for the judgement which Philebus demands.

jowett.e: And why do you suppose me to have pointed out to you the admixture which takes place in comedy? Why but to convince you that there was no difficulty in showing the mixed nature of fear and love and similar affections; and I thought that when I had given you the illustration, you would have let me off, and have acknowledged as a general truth that the body without the soul, and the soul without the body, as well as the two united, are susceptible of all sorts of admixtures of pleasures and pains; and so further discussion would have been unnecessary. And now I want to know whether I may depart; or will you keep me here until midnight? I fancy that I may obtain my release without many words;--if I promise that to-morrow I will give you an account of all these cases. But at present I would rather sail in another direction, and go to other matters which remain to be settled, before the judgment can be given which Philebus demands.

turns.e: Good, Socrates; just finish what remains in any way you please.

jowett.e: Very good, Socrates; in what remains take your own course.

turns.e: Then after the mixed pleasures we should naturally and almost of necessity proceed in turn to the unmixed.

jowett.e: Then after the mixed pleasures the unmixed should have their turn; this is the natural and necessary order.

turns.e: Very good.

jowett.e: Excellent.

turns.e: So I will turn to them and try to explain them; for I do not in the least agree with those who say that all pleasures are merely surcease from pain, but, as I said, I use them as witnesses to prove that some pleasures are apparent, but not in any way real, and that there are others which appear to be both great and numerous, but are really mixed up with pains and with cessations of the greatest pains and distresses of body and soul.

jowett.e: These, in turn, then, I will now endeavour to indicate; for with the maintainers of the opinion that all pleasures are a cessation of pain, I do not agree, but, as I was saying, I use them as witnesses, that there are pleasures which seem only and are not, and there are others again which have great power and appear in many forms, yet are intermingled with pains, and are partly alleviations of agony and distress, both of body and mind.

turns.e: But what pleasures, Socrates, may rightly be considered true?

jowett.e: Then what pleasures, Socrates, should we be right in conceiving to be true?

turns.e: Those arising from what are called beautiful colors, or from forms, most of those that arise from odors and sounds, in short all those the want of which is unfelt and painless, whereas the satisfaction furnished by them is felt by the senses, pleasant, and unmixed with pain.

jowett.e: True pleasures are those which are given by beauty of colour and form, and most of those which arise from smells; those of sound, again, and in general those of which the want is painless and unconscious, and of which the fruition is palpable to sense and pleasant and unalloyed with pain.

turns.e: Once more, Socrates, what do you mean by this?

jowett.e: Once more, Socrates, I must ask what you mean.

turns.e: My meaning is certainly not clear at the first glance, and I must try to make it so. For when I say beauty of form, I am trying to express, not what most people would understand by the words, such as the beauty of animals or of paintings, but I mean, says the argument, the straight line and the circle and the plane and solid figures formed from these by turning-lathes and rulers and patterns of angles; perhaps you understand. For I assert that the beauty of these is not relative, like that of other things, but they are always absolutely beautiful by nature and have peculiar pleasures in no way subject to comparison with the pleasures of scratching; and there are colors which possess beauty and pleasures of this character. Do you understand?

jowett.e: My meaning is certainly not obvious, and I will endeavour to be plainer. I do not mean by beauty of form such beauty as that of animals or pictures, which the many would suppose to be my meaning; but, says the argument, understand me to mean straight lines and circles, and the plane or solid figures which are formed out of them by turning-lathes and rulers and measurers of angles; for these I affirm to be not only relatively beautiful, like other things, but they are eternally and absolutely beautiful, and they have peculiar pleasures, quite unlike the pleasures of scratching. And there are colours which are of the same character, and have similar pleasures; now do you understand my meaning?

turns.e: I am trying to do so, Socrates; and I hope you also will try to make your meaning still clearer.

jowett.e: I am trying to understand, Socrates, and I hope that you will try to make your meaning clearer.

turns.e: I mean that those sounds which are smooth and clear and send forth a single pure note are beautiful, not relatively, but absolutely, and that there are pleasures which pertain to these by nature and result from them.

jowett.e: When sounds are smooth and clear, and have a single pure tone, then I mean to say that they are not relatively but absolutely beautiful, and have natural pleasures associated with them.

turns.e: Yes, that also is true.

jowett.e: Yes, there are such pleasures.

turns.e: The pleasures of smell are a less divine class; but they have no necessary pains mixed with them, and wherever and in whatever we find this freedom from pain, I regard it always as a mark of similarity to those other pleasures. These, then, are two classes of the pleasures of which I am speaking. Do you understand me?

jowett.e: The pleasures of smell are of a less ethereal sort, but they have no necessary admixture of pain; and all pleasures, however and wherever experienced, which are unattended by pains, I assign to an analogous class. Here then are two kinds of pleasures.

turns.e: I understand.

jowett.e: I understand.

turns.e: And further let us add to these the pleasures of knowledge, if they appear to us not to have hunger for knowledge or pangs of such hunger as their source.

jowett.e: To these may be added the pleasures of knowledge, if no hunger of knowledge and no pain caused by such hunger precede them.

turns.e: I agree to that.

jowett.e: And this is the case.

turns.e: Well, if men are full of knowledge and then lose it through forgetfulness, do you see any pains in the losses?

jowett.e: Well, but if a man who is full of knowledge loses his knowledge, are there not pains of forgetting?

turns.e: Not by their inherent nature, but sometimes there is pain in reflecting on the event, when a man who has lost knowledge is pained by the lack of it.

jowett.e: Not necessarily, but there may be times of reflection, when he feels grief at the loss of his knowledge.

turns.e: True, my dear fellow, but just at present we are recounting natural feelings only, not reflection.

jowett.e: Yes, my friend, but at present we are enumerating only the natural perceptions, and have nothing to do with reflection.

turns.e: Then you are right in saying that we feel no pain in the loss of knowledge.

jowett.e: In that case you are right in saying that the loss of knowledge is not attended with pain.

turns.e: Then we may say that these pleasures of knowledge are unmixed with pain and are felt not by the many but only by very few.

jowett.e: These pleasures of knowledge, then, are unmixed with pain; and they are not the pleasures of the many but of a very few.

turns.e: Yes, certainly.

jowett.e: Quite true.

turns.e: And now that we have fairly well separated the pure pleasures and those which may be pretty correctly called impure, let us add the further statement that the intense pleasures are without measure and those of the opposite sort have measure; those which admit of greatness and intensity and are often or seldom great or intense we shall assign to the class of the infinite, which circulates more or less freely through the body and soul alike, and the others we shall assign to the class of the limited.

jowett.e: And now, having fairly separated the pure pleasures and those which may be rightly termed impure, let us further add to our description of them, that the pleasures which are in excess have no measure, but that those which are not in excess have measure; the great, the excessive, whether more or less frequent, we shall be right in referring to the class of the infinite, and of the more and less, which pours through body and soul alike; and the others we shall refer to the class which has measure.

turns.e: Quite right, Socrates.

jowett.e: Quite right, Socrates.

turns.e: There is still another question about them to be considered.

jowett.e: Still there is something more to be considered about pleasures.

turns.e: What is it?

jowett.e: What is it?

turns.e: What kind of thing is most closely related to truth? The pure and unadulterated, or the violent, the widespread, the great, and the sufficient?

jowett.e: When you speak of purity and clearness, or of excess, abundance, greatness and sufficiency, in what relation do these terms stand to truth?

turns.e: What is your object, Socrates, in asking that question?

jowett.e: Why do you ask, Socrates?

turns.e: My object, Protarchus, is to leave no gap in my test of pleasure and knowledge, if some part of each of them is pure and some part impure, in order that each of them may offer itself for judgement in a condition of purity, and thus make the judgement easier for you and me and all our audience.

jowett.e: Because, Protarchus, I should wish to test pleasure and knowledge in every possible way, in order that if there be a pure and impure element in either of them, I may present the pure element for judgment, and then they will be more easily judged of by you and by me and by all of us.

turns.e: Quite right.

jowett.e: Most true.

turns.e: Very well, let us adopt that point of view towards all the classes which we call pure. First let us select one of them and examine it.

jowett.e: Let us investigate all the pure kinds; first selecting for consideration a single instance.

turns.e: Which shall we select?

jowett.e: What instance shall we select?

turns.e: Let us first, if agreeable to you, consider whiteness.

jowett.e: Suppose that we first of all take whiteness.

turns.e: By all means.

jowett.e: Very good.

turns.e: How can we have purity in whiteness, and what purity? Is it the greatest and most widespread, or the most unmixed, that in which there is no trace of any other color?

jowett.e: How can there be purity in whiteness, and what purity? Is that purest which is greatest or most in quantity, or that which is most unadulterated and freest from any admixture of other colours?

turns.e: Clearly it is the most unadulterated.

jowett.e: Clearly that which is most unadulterated.

turns.e: Right. Shall we not, then, Protarchus, declare that this, and not the most numerous or the greatest, is both the truest and the most beautiful of all whitenesses?

jowett.e: True, Protarchus; and so the purest white, and not the greatest or largest in quantity, is to be deemed truest and most beautiful?

turns.e: Quite right.

jowett.e: Right.

turns.e: Then we shall be perfectly right in saying that a little pure white is whiter and more beautiful and truer than a great deal of mixed white.

jowett.e: And we shall be quite right in saying that a little pure white is whiter and fairer and truer than a great deal that is mixed.

turns.e: Perfectly right.

turns.e: Well then, we shall have no need of many such examples in our discussion of pleasure; we see well enough from this one that any pleasure, however small or infrequent, if uncontaminated with pain, is pleasanter and more beautiful than a great or often repeated pleasure without purity.

jowett.e: There is no need of adducing many similar examples in illustration of the argument about pleasure; one such is sufficient to prove to us that a small pleasure or a small amount of pleasure, if pure or unalloyed with pain, is always pleasanter and truer and fairer than a great pleasure or a great amount of pleasure of another kind.

turns.e: Most certainly; and the example is sufficient.

jowett.e: Assuredly; and the instance you have given is quite sufficient.

turns.e: Here is another point. Have we not often heard it said of pleasure that it is always a process or generation and that there is no state or existence of pleasure? There are some clever people who try to prove this theory to us, and we ought to be grateful to them.

jowett.e: But what do you say of another question:--have we not heard that pleasure is always a generation, and has no true being? Do not certain ingenious philosophers teach this doctrine, and ought not we to be grateful to them?

turns.e: Well, what then?

jowett.e: What do they mean?

turns.e: I will explain this whole matter, Protarchus, by asking questions.

jowett.e: I will explain to you, my dear Protarchus, what they mean, by putting a question.

turns.e: Go on; ask your questions.

jowett.e: Ask, and I will answer.

turns.e: There are two parts of existence, the one self-existent, the other always desiring something else.

jowett.e: I assume that there are two natures, one self-existent, and the other ever in want of something.

turns.e: What do you mean? What are these two?

jowett.e: What manner of natures are they?

turns.e: The one is by nature more imposing, the other inferior.

jowett.e: The one majestic ever, the other inferior.

turns.e: Speak still more plainly.

jowett.e: You speak riddles.

turns.e: We have seen beloved boys who are fair and good, and brave lovers of them.

jowett.e: You have seen loves good and fair, and also brave lovers of them.

turns.e: Yes, no doubt of it.

jowett.e: I should think so.

turns.e: Try to find another pair like these in all the relations we are speaking of.

jowett.e: Search the universe for two terms which are like these two and are present everywhere.

turns.e: Must I say it a third time? Please tell your meaning more plainly, Socrates.

jowett.e: Yet a third time I must say, Be a little plainer, Socrates.

turns.e: It is no riddle, Protarchus; the talk is merely jesting with us and means that one part of existences always exists for the sake of something, and the other part is that for the sake of which the former is always coming into being.

jowett.e: There is no difficulty, Protarchus; the argument is only in play, and insinuates that some things are for the sake of something else (relatives), and that other things are the ends to which the former class subserve (absolutes).

turns.e: I can hardly understand after all your repetition.

jowett.e: Your many repetitions make me slow to understand.

turns.e: Perhaps, my boy, you will understand better as the discussion proceeds.

jowett.e: As the argument proceeds, my boy, I dare say that the meaning will become clearer.

turns.e: Let us take another pair.

jowett.e: Here are two new principles.

turns.e: What are they?

jowett.e: What are they?

turns.e: One is the generation of all things (the process of coming into being), the other is existence or being.

jowett.e: One is the generation of all things, and the other is essence.

turns.e: I accept your two, generation and being.

jowett.e: I readily accept from you both generation and essence.

turns.e: Quite right. Now which of these shall we say is for the sake of the other, generation for the sake of being, or being for the sake of generation?

jowett.e: Very right; and would you say that generation is for the sake of essence, or essence for the sake of generation?

turns.e: You are now asking whether that which is called being is what it is for the sake of generation?

jowett.e: You want to know whether that which is called essence is, properly speaking, for the sake of generation?

turns.e: Yes, plainly.

jowett.e: Yes.

turns.e: For Heaven’s sake, is this the kind of question you keep asking me, Tell me, Protarchus, whether you think shipbuilding is for the sake of ships, or ships for the sake of shipbuilding, and all that sort of thing?

jowett.e: By the gods, I wish that you would repeat your question.

turns.e: Yes; that is just what I mean, Protarchus.

jowett.e: I mean, O my Protarchus, to ask whether you would tell me that ship-building is for the sake of ships, or ships for the sake of ship-building? and in all similar cases I should ask the same question.

turns.e: Then why did you not answer it yourself, Socrates?

jowett.e: Why do you not answer yourself, Socrates?

turns.e: There is no reason why I should not; but I want you to take part in the discussion.

jowett.e: I have no objection, but you must take your part.

turns.e: Certainly.

jowett.e: Certainly.

turns.e: I say that drugs and all sorts of instruments and materials are always employed for the sake of production or generation, but that every instance of generation is for the sake of some being or other, and generation in general is for the sake of being in general.

jowett.e: My answer is, that all things instrumental, remedial, material, are given to us with a view to generation, and that each generation is relative to, or for the sake of, some being or essence, and that the whole of generation is relative to the whole of essence.

turns.e: That is very clear.

turns.e: Then pleasure, if it is a form of generation, would be generated for the sake of some form of being.

jowett.e: True.

turns.e: Now surely that for the sake of which anything is generated is in the class of the good, and that which is generated for the sake of something else, my friend, must be placed in another class.

jowett.e: And that for the sake of which something else is done must be placed in the class of good, and that which is done for the sake of something else, in some other class, my good friend.

turns.e: Most undeniably.

jowett.e: Most certainly.

turns.e: Then if pleasure is a form of generation, we shall be right in placing it in a class other than that of the good, shall we not?

jowett.e: Then pleasure, being a generation, will be rightly placed in some other class than that of good?

turns.e: Quite right.

jowett.e: Quite right.

turns.e: Then, as I said when we began to discuss this point, we ought to be grateful to him who pointed out that there is only a generation, but no existence, of pleasure; for he is clearly making a laughing-stock of those who assert that pleasure is a good.

jowett.e: Then, as I said at first, we ought to be very grateful to him who first pointed out that pleasure was a generation only, and had no true being at all; for he is clearly one who laughs at the notion of pleasure being a good.

turns.e: Yes, most emphatically.

jowett.e: Assuredly.

turns.e: And he will also surely make a laughing-stock of all those who find their highest end in forms of generation.

jowett.e: And he would surely laugh also at those who make generation their highest end.

turns.e: How is that, and to whom do you refer?

jowett.e: Of whom are you speaking, and what do they mean?

turns.e: To those who, when cured of hunger or thirst or any of the troubles which are cured by generation are pleased because of the generation, as if it were pleasure, and say that they would not wish to live without thirst and hunger and the like, if they could not experience the feelings which follow after them.

jowett.e: I am speaking of those who when they are cured of hunger or thirst or any other defect by some process of generation are delighted at the process as if it were pleasure; and they say that they would not wish to live without these and other feelings of a like kind which might be mentioned.

turns.e: That seems to be their view.

jowett.e: That is certainly what they appear to think.

turns.e: We should all agree that the opposite of generation is destruction, should we not?

jowett.e: And is not destruction universally admitted to be the opposite of generation?

turns.e: Inevitably.

jowett.e: Certainly.

turns.e: And he who chooses as they do would be choosing destruction and generation, not that third life in which there was neither pleasure nor pain, but only the purest possible thought.

jowett.e: Then he who chooses thus, would choose generation and destruction rather than that third sort of life, in which, as we were saying, was neither pleasure nor pain, but only the purest possible thought.

turns.e: It is a great absurdity, as it appears, Socrates, to tell us that pleasure is a good.

jowett.e: He who would make us believe pleasure to be a good is involved in great absurdities, Socrates.

turns.e: Yes, a great absurdity, and let us go still further.

jowett.e: Great, indeed; and there is yet another of them.

turns.e: How?

jowett.e: What is it?

turns.e: Is it not absurd to say that there is nothing good in the body or many other things, but only in the soul, and that in the soul the only good is pleasure, and that courage and self-restraint and understanding and all the other good things of the soul are nothing of the sort; and beyond all this to be obliged to say that he who is not feeling pleasure, and is feeling pain, is bad when he feels pain, though he be the best of men, and that he who feels pleasure is, when he feels pleasure, the more excellent in virtue the greater the pleasure he feels?

jowett.e: Is there not an absurdity in arguing that there is nothing good or noble in the body, or in anything else, but that good is in the soul only, and that the only good of the soul is pleasure; and that courage or temperance or understanding, or any other good of the soul, is not really a good?--and is there not yet a further absurdity in our being compelled to say that he who has a feeling of pain and not of pleasure is bad at the time when he is suffering pain, even though he be the best of men; and again, that he who has a feeling of pleasure, in so far as he is pleased at the time when he is pleased, in that degree excels in virtue?

turns.e: All that, Socrates, is the height of absurdity.

jowett.e: Nothing, Socrates, can be more irrational than all this.

turns.e: Now let us not undertake to subject pleasure to every possible test and then be found to give mind and knowledge very gentle treatment. Let us rather strike them boldly everywhere to see if their metal rings unsound at any point; so we shall find out what is by nature purest in them, and then we can make use of the truest elements of these and of pleasure to form our judgement of both.

jowett.e: And now, having subjected pleasure to every sort of test, let us not appear to be too sparing of mind and knowledge: let us ring their metal bravely, and see if there be unsoundness in any part, until we have found out what in them is of the purest nature; and then the truest elements both of pleasure and knowledge may be brought up for judgment.

turns.e: Right.

turns.e: Take music first; it is full of this; it attains harmony by guesswork based on practice, not by measurement; and flute music throughout tries to find the pitch of each note as it is produced by guess, so that the amount of uncertainty mixed up in it is great, and the amount of certainty small.

jowett.e: Music, for instance, is full of this empiricism; for sounds are harmonized, not by measure, but by skilful conjecture; the music of the flute is always trying to guess the pitch of each vibrating note, and is therefore mixed up with much that is doubtful and has little which is certain.

jowett.e: There is a wide difference between them, Protarchus; some arithmeticians reckon unequal units; as for example, two armies, two oxen, two very large things or two very small things. turns.e: My question, dear Protarchus, was not as yet what art or science surpasses all others by being the greatest and best and most useful to us: what I am trying to find out at present is which art, however little and of little use, has the greatest regard for clearness, exactness, and truth. See; you will not make Gorgias angry if you grant that his art is superior for the practical needs of men, but say that the study of which I spoke is superior in the matter of the most perfect truth, just as I said in speaking about the white that if it was small and pure it was superior to that which was great but impure. Now, therefore, with careful thought and due consideration, paying attention neither to the usefulness nor to the reputation of any arts or sciences, but to that faculty of our souls, if such there be, which by its nature loves the truth and does all things for the sake of the truth, let us examine this faculty and say whether it is most likely to possess mind and intelligence in the greatest purity, or we must look for some other faculty which has more valid claims.

jowett.e: Dear Protarchus, I never asked which was the greatest or best or usefullest of arts or sciences, but which had clearness and accuracy, and the greatest amount of truth, however humble and little useful an art. And as for Gorgias, if you do not deny that his art has the advantage in usefulness to mankind, he will not quarrel with you for saying that the study of which I am speaking is superior in this particular of essential truth; as in the comparison of white colours, a little whiteness, if that little be only pure, was said to be superior in truth to a great mass which is impure. And now let us give our best attention and consider well, not the comparative use or reputation of the sciences, but the power or faculty, if there be such, which the soul has of loving the truth, and of doing all things for the sake of it; let us search into the pure element of mind and intelligence, and then we shall be able to say whether the science of which I have been speaking is most likely to possess the faculty, or whether there be some other which has higher claims.

turns.e: I am considering, and I think it is difficult to concede that any other science or art cleaves more closely to truth than this.

Is not and was not that what was said, Protarchus?
